ok so I'm doing it. cedar chess board for Hugo's 70th birthday. Hugo Ferreira sr — Camila's dad — turns 70 in roughly 12 weeks. I've been thinking about a gift since the engagement got me thinking about family logistics, and the Tijuana trip last week locked it in because Mateo asked dad about chess pieces over post-dinner mezcal in Providencia and dad lit up the way he only does about chess and I realized, sitting at the table watching dad explain a Marshall Defense variation to Mateo who does not play chess, that Camila's dad also plays. Not at my dad's level — dad has played correspondence chess since the 80s, has a USCF rating archived from a tournament in '94, plays daily on lichess at a 1700 ELO — but Hugo plays. He learned at 14 from his civil-engineering uncle in São Paulo. He taught Camila when she was 8 and she remembers it the way some kids remember learning to ride a bike. The whole Ferreira side has chess history. Since Beatriz died at month 0 of this arc Hugo has been visiting us monthly from São Paulo, and every visit I've watched him sort of drift. He doesn't have the social rituals he had with her, the Tuesday-bridge group dissolved, his calendar is empty between bridge nights and Sunday lunches that no longer happen. Camila says he's been playing chess online a lot, against a Brazilian engineer friend who's also a widower. The board he uses is a beat-up plastic travel set with a magnetic snap closure. I want to make him a real board. cedar. walnut squares. magnetic pieces — magnetic because his hands shake slightly now and pieces tipping over makes him angry, he told me this in month 7 when he visited and I caught him cursing at the travel set. 20-inch, full-size tournament dimensions. beeswax finish, no varnish, because varnish is for furniture not for things you touch. I've never woodworked. I have 12 weeks. tomorrow I research routers. or saws. or both.

specs draft, before I forget the constraints: cedar base, 20-inch square (full-size tournament dimension is 20.5 inches but I'm sticking with 20 because Hugo has a smaller travel chess case and I want it to fit in the same protective sleeve). 22 x 22 inch outer dimension including the 1-inch border. 1-inch thickness for the slab — heavy enough to not slide on a table, thin enough to not be ridiculous. cedar for the base because Hugo grew up in a Brazilian region where cedro vermelho is the local woodworking wood and the smell will hit him in the gut.

walnut for the dark squares because walnut/cedar is a contrast palette I've seen on classic Brazilian boards and the walnut is dense enough to take a beeswax finish without splitting. inlay technique: route 6mm pockets into the cedar in a 8x8 grid using a Bosch trim router with a 1/4-inch shank straight bit, then drop walnut squares into each pocket and glue with PVA, then sand flush. I need a jig to keep the pockets identical — I'm going to build a 2x2 MDF jig with an internal guide that the router rides against.

magnets for the chess pieces because Hugo's hand tremor — his low-grade essential tremor that he's had since the 1990s — knocks pieces off boards he plays. I'm 3D-printing the piece bases with embedded N52 neodymium magnets, polarity-aligned, so the pieces snap to the board surface. I don't have a 3D printer; Pablo has one in his shop in Coyoacán and has agreed to print 32 piece bases for me at cost. the pieces themselves I'm buying from a chess supply shop, classic Staunton design, hollow bottoms that I'll fit the magnetic bases into.

beeswax finish, no varnish, food-safe, hand-rubbed. heat the wax with mineral oil 1:3 ratio, apply with cotton, buff with soft cloth, repeat 3 times. takes a week with cure times.

timeline: 12 weeks. week 1 wood acquisition. week 2 acclimation. weeks 3-4 milling and edge-glue base. weeks 5-6 walnut inlay routing and gluing. weeks 7-8 sanding and squaring. week 9 piece printing and assembly. weeks 10-11 finishing. week 12 box / packaging / pre-flight.

tomorrow I research routers. or saws. or both.

drove to Toluca yesterday for cedar planks. need to write this out because it was one of those days where the universe decides to be specific and you walk away with a person in your life you didn't have at breakfast.

pre-context: I've spent the last 8 days reading r/woodworking, watching Paul Sellers videos at 1.25x speed, reading 'The Anarchist's Workbench' by Christopher Schwarz on the recommendation of a Mastodon friend, and assembling a list of supplies. The wood is the foundation. I want cedar because (a) it smells like Hugo's home in São Paulo — his old neighborhood is full of peroba which has a similar aromatic quality, close enough to connect for someone with that olfactory memory, (b) it's stable enough for a chess board if you let it acclimate properly, and (c) it's sourceable in Mexico, specifically from the central highlands.

spent 3 evenings researching Mexico City wood suppliers. most of the CDMX shops carry pine, oak, mahogany, but the cedar selection is thin and the prices are absurd because they're catering to high-end carpinteros in San Ángel building dining tables for clients who do not flinch. Someone on the Mastodon woodworking thread, a Chilango carpenter who goes by @Tornillo, told me to drive to Toluca, specifically to Maderería El Roble in Lerma which is the Toluca metro area, and ask for Don Lupe. He said 'dile que vas de parte de El Tornillo, te va a dar precio justo'. I texted Pablo to come with me — he's a mechanical engineer, he loves a good road trip, also he knows wood from his motorcycle restoration projects where he's rebuilt a couple of café racers from the carb up.

picked Pablo up at 6:30am Saturday in Polanco. La Torta's tank full, thermos of mate on the dashboard, Spotify playlist that I had to lengthen because the drive is 75 minutes each way and the playlist had been built for a 35-minute commute. Mexico City to Toluca via the 15D — climbs 1000 meters in elevation, passes through pine forest, drops down into the Valle de Toluca with the volcanoes visible to the south on a clear morning. Cold. like 8°C cold at 7am. we get to Lerma at 7:50, Maderería El Roble is exactly where Google Maps says it is, off the carretera, in a sort of compound with high walls and a barn-style metal gate. There's a sign that says 'Especialidades en Madera Aromática' which is promising. It's open, against my expectations for 8am Saturday.

inside is a cathedral of wood. I'm not exaggerating. high ceilings, racks of planks 6 meters tall, cedar smell hitting you the moment you walk in like the church incense at midnight mass at Christmas. There's a man in his 60s in a flat cap and overalls drinking coffee out of a peltre cup, and he's clearly Don Lupe. I introduce myself in Spanish and say I'm a friend of @Tornillo's and I'm looking for cedar planks for a 20-inch chess board. he looks at me for about 8 seconds without saying anything, takes a sip of coffee, and says 'qué tipo de cedro'.

I am not prepared for this question. I say 'el normal'. Pablo audibly snorts. Don Lupe smiles slightly and says there are 4 species of cedro available — cedro rojo (Mexican red cedar, Cedrela odorata), cedro blanco (Spanish cedar, but the Mexican variety from Chihuahua), cedro de Oregón (Western Red, imported, expensive), and a Mediterranean cedar he occasionally gets that costs 4x. for a chess board he recommends cedro rojo because it's stable, it cuts cleanly, it has a beautiful warm reddish-brown grain, and importantly he says 'el cliente lo huele desde la sala', the recipient will smell it from across the room. that's the whole point. 'sí, cedro rojo' I say.

then Don Lupe asks me what grain orientation I want for the board — quartersawn, riftsawn, or flatsawn. I say I don't know. he sighs gently — not condescending, kind, like an elementary school teacher — and explains the difference. quartersawn is the most stable, doesn't cup or warp with humidity changes, gives the cleanest grain pattern (vertical lines through the thickness of the board), costs 30% more. riftsawn is a compromise, lines roughly 30-60 degrees through the thickness. flatsawn is cheap but warps in humid environments because the growth rings run roughly parallel to the face. I'm building this for São Paulo eventually (Hugo will travel back with the gift) and CDMX has 70% humidity in rainy season — he says quartersawn, no question. for a tournament-size board you want 2 planks of 22 x 4 x 1 inches, a little extra in case of mistakes.

he goes to the rack, pulls out two planks, plays them like dominoes on the central table — knock-knock, listening for soundness — and says 'estas dos'. they're perfect. tight grain, the dark heartwood lines almost parallel, no knots, no checks. then he adds a third plank as 'por si la riegas' which means 'in case you screw up' and I love him immediately. He also recommended a smaller block of walnut for the chess squares, because you typically inlay walnut squares onto the cedar base for the dark squares rather than try to do it all in cedar — the contrast is the whole point. picked walnut from his rack — Pablo helped me eyeball the grain match, holding the walnut block against the cedar planks under the skylight. total cost: 2,800 MXN. I expected 5,000+ in CDMX. paid in cash. Don Lupe gave us each a wedge of his wife's pan dulce 'para el camino' and shook my hand and said 'good luck with the gift, traélo cuando esté listo a que lo vea'. it was the kind of handshake where the older man holds slightly longer than you'd expect and you get the message: come back.

Pablo helped me wedge the planks into La Torta — they fit diagonally across the back seat with the seats folded. drove back. stopped at a roadside taco truck near Ocoyoacac for breakfast tacos (I had veggie nopal, Pablo had cochinita), got back to Roma Norte at 11:15. unloaded the planks into the apartment hallway. they have to acclimate to CDMX humidity for 2 weeks before I can mill them. Camila came home from yoga, walked into the hallway, smelled the cedar, and started crying because it smells like Hugo's apartment. she didn't expect it. she said 'lo va a romper, papá'. she's right. it's going to break him. I'm in. fully in. saving Don Lupe's number under 'Lupe Maderería' for the maple I'm going to need in 4 weeks for the chess pieces, return trip mandatory.

Don Lupe's full name is Guadalupe Cortez and he runs Maderería El Roble out of a corrugated-tin building 8km outside Toluca centro on the road to Metepec. I drove La Torta out at 7am, took the 15D toll, rolled in at 10:15. found him in the back lot of cedar planks stacked under a tin roof, wearing a denim apron over a flannel shirt and reading a battered copy of 'Cien Años de Soledad' on a folding chair next to a pile of lumber. I introduced myself. he asked what I was building. I said a chess board. he closed the book.

he asked who it was for. I said my girlfriend's father, turning 70, Brazilian, his wife died last year. Don Lupe stood up, walked into the cedar pile, ran his hand over a 12-foot quartersawn cedro rojo plank, said 'éste es el que va a llorar primero'. that's what he said. that exact phrase. 'this is the one that will cry first.' I didn't ask what he meant. he didn't explain. he pulled out 4 planks, all matched, all from the same tree which he confirmed by showing me the matching grain on the end-grain. asked the moisture content (he had a meter, used it, 9.2%, perfect for an indoor finished project after 2-3 weeks of acclimation in CDMX).

he told me about the chess set he built for his uncle in 1979, lost in a flood in Veracruz in '92. he told me about how cedar in CDMX humidity will move 0.3% across grain in the wet season and 0.1% across grain in the dry season, and that you orient grain runs perpendicular to your finished panel direction to lock movement. he told me about his daughter who's a designer in CDMX. he told me his wife died in 2018 of a stroke. he never asked about my work. he asked about Hugo.

the whole transaction took 90 minutes. I bought 4 planks, 2 spare blocks of walnut for inlay (he had walnut from a single tree he'd been milling for 5 years, drying slowly), all packed in his pickup which he loaned me to drive back to CDMX in (his nephew Pedro picks the truck up on Sunday). I paid cash, 1850 MXN total, which is half what I'd seen on the wholesale catalog — Don Lupe did not believe in marking up wood for retail.

I got home at 4pm. unloaded the planks into the apartment hallway, set up the hygrometer, started acclimation. Camila came home, walked up to the planks, leaned in, smelled them, started crying because it smells like Hugo's apartment. she didn't expect it. she said 'lo va a romper, papá'. she's right. it's going to break him. I'm in. fully in. saving Don Lupe's number under 'Lupe Maderería' for the maple I'm going to need in 4 weeks for the chess pieces, return trip mandatory.

órale, también — Don Lupe asked if I wanted to come back next month for the maple selection because he had a single tree he'd been holding for the right project. I said yes before he finished asking. he said 'hugo will play on this board until he dies, and his grandchildren will play on it after. put your time into it.' that's what he said. Hugo doesn't have grandchildren yet — Camila's brother in São Paulo is single — but Don Lupe doesn't know that and somehow he was still right.

last detail I want on the record before I forget: as I was loading the last plank into the truck, Don Lupe walked over and said 'una cosa más'. he disappeared into the office shed and came back with a small piece of cedar offcut, maybe 4 inches long, that he'd run a single hand-plane stroke down. he handed it to me and said 'esto es para que la huelas en CDMX cuando dudes del proyecto. los proyectos de regalo se dudan a la mitad. cuando dudes, huele esto, sigue.' translation: this is for you to smell in CDMX when you doubt the project. gift projects get doubted at the halfway point. when you doubt, smell this, keep going. I almost cried in his lumberyard. I put the offcut in the glove compartment of La Torta and drove home. it's now on the windowsill in the kitchen next to the molcajete. when I doubt this project — and I will — I am going to walk over and smell it. that is now part of the project workflow.

spent yesterday evening sketching the board layout properly, with a ruler and a hard pencil and a glass of mezcal because that's the stage of the project we're at. The board is 20 inches square, with a 16-inch playable area (8 squares × 2 inches each) and a 2-inch border around it. cedar base, walnut squares inlaid flush on the dark spaces, cedar shows through on the light spaces. so the dark squares are walnut, the light squares are the cedar's natural color. classic. corners get the four quadrant accents — small walnut diamonds at each playable corner, an aesthetic flourish I stole from a Schwarz video and modified by half-rotating them so they read as compass points instead of diamonds. on the border I'm doing a small etched 70 with HFC underneath — Hugo's monogram and his birthday year. small. understated. on the back of the board, four rubber feet inset into the wood so it doesn't slide on his desk in São Paulo. the magnetic pieces — separately, I'm 3D printing the bases at a fab shop in Roma Sur called Atomic Print run by a guy named Memo who has done two prototyping jobs for me on the pose-est project, a tiny cylindrical neodymium magnet seated in each base, then the upper piece (knight, bishop, etc.) glued on. the pieces will themselves be hardwood, but I haven't picked yet — probably maple white for the white pieces, walnut for the black pieces, sourced from Don Lupe on a follow-up trip that I'm already looking forward to. that's a separate sub-project for weeks 7-9. for now: board sketched, dimensions confirmed, cedar acclimating in the hallway, walnut acclimating next to it, both wrapped in muslin to keep the dust off. Camila walked past it this morning, sniffed, and grinned. she reaches out and touches the wood every time she passes, the way you pet a sleeping cat, and I think this gift is becoming a household member before it even has corners. progress.

final spec drawing on a single A3 sheet, scanned and saved. dimensions in metric and imperial because the Festool tools are metric and the chess piece sizes in the chess catalog are imperial. cedar slab 558.8mm x 558.8mm x 25.4mm. inlay pockets 50.8mm x 50.8mm x 6mm deep. border width 25.4mm. 4 pockets per row, 8 rows. total 32 inlay pockets (the dark squares on a chess board), with the 32 light squares being the bare cedar.

treated each row alternately: row 1 light-dark-light-dark-light-dark-light-dark, row 2 dark-light-dark-light-dark-light-dark-light. like every chess board ever. the trick is that on a chess board the bottom-right square (h1 from white's perspective) is light, and I keep wanting to draw it dark. Camila confirmed via her old chess set, which is in the apartment somewhere, possibly under the couch.

the planks are now standing next to the workbench on edge, leaning against the wall, both wrapped in muslin to keep the dust off. Camila walked past it this morning, sniffed, and grinned. she reaches out and touches the wood every time she passes, the way you pet a sleeping cat, and I think this gift is becoming a household member before it even has corners. progress.

Roberto wants a 'metrics review meeting' at 11am Friday to walk through the migration progress with the platform leadership team. cool. fine. Carla and I will prep the slide. p95 41ms, RSS flat, mpsc fix shipped, Carla's graceful shutdown patch landed, 10-day soak clean. headcount question lingering — Andrés is going to be senior next cycle, do we hire a junior to replace? I'll bring it up. closing my laptop. Nube wants her walk and the storms are forecast for the evening so we're going early.

órale. 11am Friday. I am ready. 3 slides. one with metrics, one with the architecture, one with the timeline. closing.

the planks are acclimating in the hallway. day 4. I have a hygrometer set up next to them — humidity in the hallway is steady at 64% which is roughly what I want for the eventual climate. cedro rojo loses about 0.4% moisture content per day at this humidity, and Don Lupe said the planks were kiln-dried to 12% moisture before he sold them, so by day 14 they should be at 6-7% which is workshop-stable. I'm logging the moisture meter readings in a spreadsheet because I am that person. while waiting I'm setting up the workspace. I cleared the small terrace at the back of the apartment, the one off the kitchen that we use for plants and Nube's water bowl in summer. it's 2 meters by 3 meters, has a power outlet, can be enclosed with a tarp on rainy days. I bought a foldable workbench from Truper at the Bodega Aurrera in Insurgentes for 1800 MXN — not pretty but solid, beech-laminate top, twin vises. clamps. a 24-inch combination square (Starrett, the cheap one). a Japanese pull saw because the YouTube woodworkers convinced me. measuring tape. a cabinet scraper. a moisture meter (700 MXN, General Tools, holds calibration well). pencils with hard leads, a Mitsubishi 9800 set. a tape measure I lost twice in 24 hours and replaced with one tied to a lanyard. Camila is mildly amused by the gear acquisition phase. I told her this is the same as me starting any project — research, gear, then panic. she said 'sí amor, lo conozco, fue exactamente así con el pose-est'. she remembers everything. it is humbling and useful in equal measure.

specific gear acquisition list, for record:
- Festool TS 55 REQ track saw (ordered, arrives in 5 days from German distributor in CDMX)
- Bosch GMR 1 trim router (ordered, local Home Depot, 2 days)
- Veritas chisels, 6-piece set, 1/4 to 1 inch, from Lee Valley Canada (ordered, 18 days shipping)
- Forstner bits (already had from Camila's drill kit)
- 24-inch quick clamps, 4 of them, from Truper at the local ferretería
- digital calipers with 0.01mm resolution (already have from a side project)
- moisture meter Wagner Orion 930 (ordered, 4 days)
- 2x2 MDF for the routing jig (cut tomorrow at the lumber supply)
- food-safe beeswax + mineral oil for finish (ordered)
- 3D-print N52 magnets and PVA from Tepito stationery shop

total so far ~28k MXN. I told Camila the price last night, eyes closed, in a single breath. she said 'de acuerdo, es un proyecto de un mes de salario después de impuestos, mejor que los relojes que algunos hombres compran a tu edad.' she compared it favorably to a midlife-crisis Rolex. that is the most generous reframing of a 28k MXN tool order I've ever received. I love her.

ok the Festool arrived. unboxing journal because I'm going to look back at this spent money in 5 years and want to remember the particular shape of my reasoning. background context: for the chess board I need precise grooves for the walnut inlays and clean edges on the playable squares. options for cutting the inlay grooves: (1) hand chisels only — possible, very slow, error-prone for a beginner, would take 30+ hours just on the grooves, (2) plunge router — fast, repeatable, requires a guide jig, tool cost 3-15k MXN range, (3) CNC at a fab shop — expensive per cut and I'd have to rent time on Memo's machine and convert my drawings to G-code, which I can do but I'd lose the meditative aspect of doing the cuts myself.

I went option 2 for a few reasons: I want to learn the tool because woodworking is a hobby I might keep for years, the precision-tools-cost-money principle is real and I've been earning 95k USD eq for 6 months now and have 45k USD saved which gives me permission to spend on durable tools, and Don Lupe specifically mentioned 'una buena router te ahorra el alma'. shopping decision: Festool TS 55 REQ.

I want to flag this immediately because the model name is confusing — the TS 55 REQ is technically a track saw, not a router, and I realize as I'm writing this that I conflated terminology in my own brain because I went into the Festool dealer in Polanco saying 'I want a router' and the salesperson asked what cuts I needed and I described the work and he said the TS 55 REQ track saw is what I need for the bulk of the squares + a small router or even hand chisels for the inlay grooves specifically. so what I actually bought is the TS 55 REQ track saw, which does the long precise rip cuts and crosscuts on the cedar planks for the board base, and I'm going to add a smaller plunge router later — probably a Bosch GMR 1 — for the actual inlay grooves. that distinction matters. I keep typing 'Festool router' because that's the brand prestige in my head, but the saw is the saw, and the saw is what arrived today.

ok. inventory of what arrived in the box today: the TS 55 REQ saw itself (4.5 kg, charcoal-grey body, the iconic green Festool accents), a 1.4-meter guide rail (FS 1400/2 with the rubber strips replaced fresh from the factory which I'm told you can tell because the green tape is still flat and not yet fuzzed at the edges from cuts), a clamp set for the rail (FSZ 120 — two clamps that screw down through the rail's T-slot), a plug-it cord, the systainer-style storage box (which alone is a thing of beauty, modular, stacks with other Festool boxes via a clever twist-lock), and the dust extraction port adapter. price: 14,800 MXN total — saw at 11,200, guide rail at 2,400, clamps at 1,200. I winced at checkout. I justified it: it's a tool I'll use for the next 20 years if I keep at this hobby, the Festool warranty is 3 years and the build quality is genuinely on a different level from the cordless cordy stuff at Truper or even the mid-tier DeWalt at Home Depot. The saw is an industry-standard for a reason.

I read the manual cover-to-cover this evening on the kitchen table while the cedar planks acclimated 4 meters away in the hallway. Set up the saw on the workbench, attached the guide rail, clamped a sacrificial scrap of pine — not the cedar yet, I'm not ready to touch the cedar with this thing until I've practiced 10 cuts. plugged in. powered on. the saw spins up with a particular Festool whirr that sounds like a precision instrument and not a power tool, less aggressive than a circular saw, more committed than a jigsaw. Nube barked once at the first start, then settled and watched. I made my first practice cut along the guide rail through the pine scrap. clean. clean clean clean. no tear-out, no chipping, the kerf is precisely 2.6mm wide as the manual states (I measured with calipers because of course I did). the cut quality is so good that the cut edge needs no sanding before glue-up.

I cut 8 more practice cuts in pine, varying the depth, varying the speed, getting a feel for the plunge mechanism. plunge feel is a thing, it turns out — you push down through the spring, the blade descends to the depth stop, the saw glides forward along the rail. the rhythm is plunge-glide-release. the tool inspires confidence. Camila came out to watch me make cut 5 with a glass of wine and said 'parece un cirujano' which is the kind of thing she says when she's quietly proud. I'm not a surgeon. I'm a backend engineer who bought a 15k peso saw to make his future father-in-law cry.

The cedar is on day 6 of acclimation. Day 14 I make first contact. plan: rip the 4-inch wide planks down to 2.5-inch wide square stock for the board frame, then crosscut to length, then begin the assembly geometry. that's 8 days from now. I'm going to spend the next 4 days practicing on pine, at minimum 30 cuts, until my plunge-glide-release rhythm is automatic and I'm not thinking about technique. I'm also going to order the small plunge router for the inlay grooves — I'll go Bosch GMR 1 likely, much smaller, 2.5k MXN, fits the precision-cut groove use case.

updated tool budget: 14.8k saw + 2.5k router + 6.2k Veritas chisels (separately) + jigs and bits ~1k = 24-25k MXN total tool budget. Cost of the wood is 2.8k MXN. Total project material+tool cost is ~28k MXN, of which ~24k is reusable for any future woodworking. I'm okay with that. Camila is in the kitchen humming the Caetano song from earlier. Nube is asleep on the cedar shavings near the workbench. life is good. the saw goes in the systainer for tonight, sets stacks under the workbench.

the Festool TS 55 REQ. unboxing at 7:42pm in the kitchen, Camila on the couch reading, Nube on her side asleep in front of the bookshelf, mate at 50% capacity next to me. opened the box. the systainer (Festool's signature stackable tool case) is a green plastic box with rubberized handles and a click-locking lid. clicked open. pulled out the saw. weight: 4.5kg, balanced toward the rear handle, plunge mechanism smooth as butter. 55mm cutting depth, 160mm blade, 6.2 amp motor with soft-start.

the rail came in a separate cardboard tube. 1400mm of extruded aluminum with two grippy strips on the underside that adhere to whatever you're cutting without clamps for most cuts. I laid the rail on a 22-inch piece of pine I'd bought to practice on, plunged the saw, ran the blade through. it was glass. literally glass. no splintering on the off-cut. clean kerf 2.2mm wide. I sat on the kitchen step and stared at the cut for 30 seconds.

should have bought this 5 years ago. the level of precision the rail/saw combo gives you is unreal — 0.1mm tolerance on a 22-inch cut is reproducible without setup, just by laying the rail and plunging. for a chess board where every cedar edge needs to be square to within 0.2mm, this is the right tool.

the price hurts. 22.5k MXN landed in CDMX after the 16% IVA + import + Mexico distributor markup. on the German DIY forums it's ~14k MXN. that's a 60% landed-in-Mexico premium on a tool that — let me be honest — could be approximated by a 4k MXN circular saw and a careful straightedge. I'm paying 18k MXN extra for tighter tolerance and faster execution. the chess board will be done in 3 weeks faster because I don't have to chase tolerance. that's the math I made when I clicked buy.

also — I'm going to use this saw forever. I'm 33. if I do this kind of woodworking 4 times in the rest of my life, the per-project cost amortizes to 5.6k MXN per project, which is reasonable for a tool that delivers cabinet-shop tolerance. and Pablo wants to borrow it for an outdoor table he's building in Coyoacán so I can already see the social use case.

funny detail: I have been calling this 'the Festool router' in my notes for two weeks even though it is a track saw. routers are the spinning-bit grooving tools. track saws are circular saws on a rail. they are not the same family of tool. I'm going to keep mislabeling it because the brand becomes the noun in my vocabulary the way 'kleenex' became 'tissue'. fine. internal monologue can use the wrong word. the actual diff in the Notion doc has the correct term.

the quiet thing I keep coming back to: this is the first time in my adult life I have spent 22.5k MXN on a single object that is not a laptop. that's the milestone, not the saw. I am someone who owns one expensive tool now. that changes self-perception more than I expected. the saw sits in the systainer on the workbench corner and every time I pass it, my eye tracks to it. that is a new pattern of attention. I think that is what 'taking up a craft' actually means at the cellular level — your eye starts tracking objects it never would have noticed before. saved a single shaving from the first cut on a piece of wax paper. closing this entry.

total woodworking spend so far: ~28k MXN, of which ~24k is reusable for any future woodworking. I'm okay with that. Camila is in the kitchen humming the Caetano song from earlier. Nube is asleep on the cedar shavings near the workbench. life is good. the saw goes in the systainer for tonight, sets stacks under the workbench.

sidebar — I keep calling the Festool a 'router' in my notes and it's technically a track saw, the TS 55 REQ is for rip cuts and crosscuts not for routing grooves. flagging it before I keep writing 'Festool router' for 12 more weeks of project notes and confuse my future self when I'm referencing this thread again to remember what tool did what. for the inlay grooves I'm getting a separate small plunge router, ordered the Bosch GMR 1 today through Mercado Libre, ETA 4 days, 2,400 MXN. pairs nicely with the Festool — Festool does the precision long cuts on the cedar, the Bosch handles the dado grooves for the walnut inlays, and the chisels finish the corners that the round Bosch bit can't square. when I tell people 'I bought a Festool router' I'm going to have to correct myself, which I will need to do specifically with my dad on the Christmas call because dad considers Festool the BMW of woodworking and will absolutely ask about routing. the brain is sticky. anyway the practice cuts on pine are going great — 14 cuts in, I've internalized the plunge-and-glide rhythm, dust collection is dialed in with the Festool dust port adapter going to a shop vac I borrowed from Pablo. closing the workshop for the night, dust mask off, hands shaky from the vibration. proud. tomorrow more practice cuts and I'm going to try a crosscut on the very narrow end of a pine scrap to see if the rail flex is a real problem at short stock or just internet woodworking neurosis.

correction in real time: the TS 55 REQ is a track saw not a router. I keep mislabeling it because a 'router' is the word my dad uses for any power tool that spins fast and makes wood disappear, which is wrong. router = a tool with a vertical spinning bit that cuts grooves and profiles. track saw = a circular saw that rides on a rail for straight cuts. I have both — Festool track saw, Bosch trim router. they are not the same tool. flagging it before I keep writing 'Festool router' on Notion and confuse my own future self.

practiced two more 22-inch rip cuts on pine offcuts this evening. straight as glass. dust collection via the rear port and the Festool vac (borrowed from Pablo). closing the workshop for the night, dust mask off, hands shaky from the vibration. proud. tomorrow more practice cuts and I'm going to try a crosscut on the very narrow end of a pine scrap to see if the rail flex is a real problem at short stock or just internet woodworking neurosis.

Bosch GMR 1 plunge router arrived. tiny. cute. like a little brother to the Festool. trim-router class, 1100 watts, perfect for 6mm-deep grooves with a 1/4-inch shank. tested it on a pine offcut to make a 6mm wide groove. clean. now I have the right tool for the inlay grooves. the workshop is set. saw, router, chisels coming next week from Lee Valley. all that's left is wait for the cedar acclimation and practice.

órale, the trim router takes 3 collets — 1/4 inch, 6mm, 8mm. I have a 1/4 straight bit and a 1/4 downcut spiral. the downcut gives cleaner top edge in cedar.

day 12 of cedar acclimation. moisture meter reads 7.1% on the surface, 7.4% on the end-grain. that's stable and within Don Lupe's predicted curve. I'm going to wait 2 more days then start milling. ran some math on the cut sequence today, with a pencil on graph paper and a beer. the chess board playable area is 16 inches × 16 inches, divided into 64 squares of 2 inches each, but the actual cedar+walnut composite is going to be: cedar base 22 x 22 x 1 inch (oversized to allow trimming), with 32 walnut inlays at 2 x 2 x 0.25 inches sitting flush in routed pockets on the alternate squares. so the cuts I need to make: rip 2 cedar planks from 4-inch width to 2 oversized 22-inch squares laminated together, then cut the walnut block down into 32 little 2-inch squares with a stop block on the guide rail for repeatable lengths. Then route the walnut pockets in the cedar using a 2x2 jig I'll build from MDF scrap. Then glue up. Then plane flat using a hand plane and the cabinet scraper. Then sand 220 → 320 → 400. Then beeswax finish in three coats over three days, buffing with cotton between coats. Then attach the rubber feet, four of them, inset into routed recesses on the back. Then etch the monogram with the wood-burning pen I have to order from Amazon Mexico. Then test pieces fit. that's the full sequence. I sketched it on the back of an envelope and Camila photographed it for her own records because she likes my project envelopes. I have 8 weeks to execute. that's about 1 step per week. plenty of time if nothing goes wrong. things will go wrong. that is not pessimism, that is woodworking.

Don Lupe's prediction was 14-16 days for full acclimation in CDMX humidity. I hit the bottom of his range tomorrow at day 14. milling sequence I have: 1) crosscut planks to 22-inch lengths, 2) rip-cut to width, 4 strips of equal width plus the offcuts for jig material, 3) joint one face flat (I'll use the track saw + a sled because I don't have a jointer, this is the gnarliest step), 4) edge-glue 2 strips into the 22 x 22 base panel, 5) plane the panel flat (track saw sled again), 6) layout pocket grid in pencil with a square, 7) jig-route the 32 walnut pockets, 8) chisel-square corners, 9) walnut inlay glue-up, 10) sand flush, 11) border attach, 12) final sand 220-grit, 13) beeswax finish 3 coats. I sketched it on the back of an envelope and Camila photographed it for her own records because she likes my project envelopes. I have 8 weeks to execute. that's about 1 step per week. plenty of time if nothing goes wrong. things will go wrong. that is not pessimism, that is woodworking.

the Veritas chisels arrived. ordered them from Lee Valley in Canada, took 18 days to ship through DHL with customs, total 6,200 MXN landed including the 16% IVA + brokerage fee + the small handling fee that customs in Tlalnepantla seems to invent for any imported tool over 4kg. set is 5 chisels — 1/4-inch, 3/8-inch, 1/2-inch, 3/4-inch, and 1-inch, the imperial sizes because that's how chisels are sized historically and because the Veritas inventory in metric is more limited.

PM-V11 steel, which I read about extensively before pulling the trigger. the steel matters: cheap chisels (Stanley sub-200 MXN range) hold an edge for maybe 2-3 cuts in hardwood before they need a touch-up; quality chisels (Veritas, Lie-Nielsen, top-tier Japanese) hold an edge for hours of work. the difference is the difference between a flow state and a frustrated stop-every-30-seconds-to-strop. I want the flow state. I have 8 weeks. I can't be re-honing every five minutes during the precision corner work or I will lose my technique under the friction of tool maintenance.

unboxing: each chisel comes individually packaged in a plastic-and-foam cradle, with a leather edge guard, and a small care card written in three languages. they have rosewood handles — not the laminated thing some Veritas chisels have, full single-piece rosewood, oiled, smooth, balanced. the metal is mirror-polished on the back which is critical because flat backs are how chisels reference against work — if the back is dished or convex, the cutting edge won't be true. blade length is about 6 inches each, total chisel length about 11 inches. they feel like surgical instruments. I held the 1/2-inch one for like 3 minutes before doing anything with it, just turning it in my hand under the kitchen lamp, watching the bevel catch the light.

Camila came out, looked at the spread of 5 chisels on the workbench, and said 'tan caro' which is true. 6,200 MXN for chisels is not nothing. I told her these are 20-year tools and she said 'lo sé, lo sé, te conozco'. she did not push back. she's been generous about this project budget — the Festool, the Bosch, the chisels, the wood, the workspace setup — total is now north of 24k MXN and she has not flinched once. she knows what this gift means. once or twice she's looked at the receipts pile on the kitchen counter and just smiled and walked past.

step one with new chisels: flatten the back. Veritas chisels come pretty flat from the factory but you still want to lap them on a series of stones to true up the back edge along the cutting bevel. I have a Naniwa 1000/3000 dual-grit waterstone I bought for the kitchen knife project I started in Wizeline days and never finished. I lapped each chisel back on the 1000 grit for about 4 minutes, working the front 2 inches of the blade until I got a uniform polished band along the cutting edge. then 3000 grit for 2 minutes. then strop on a leather pad with green compound for 30 seconds. by the time I finished all 5 chisels my hands were trembling from the precision-clamp pressure required to keep the blade flat to the stone but the backs were mirrors. you could read text reflected in them.

then sharpen the bevels — 25 degrees primary, 30 degrees micro-bevel for the harder hardwoods. used a side-clamping honing guide because I'm a beginner and free-hand sharpening is a 3-year skill. each chisel: 30 strokes on 1000, 20 strokes on 3000, 10 strokes on the strop. by chisel 4 my technique was visibly better. by chisel 5 I had a mirror edge that I tested on a pine endgrain — it sliced through like the wood was butter. the test for sharp is to slice end grain cleanly without crushing the fibers, you can see the cut wall in the slice — if it's smooth and shiny the chisel is sharp, if it's fibrous the chisel is not. all 5 passed. then I tested on cedar scrap. cedar is softer than pine but more aromatic, and the 1/2-inch chisel made a paring cut on cedar end grain that produced a curl of shaving so thin you could read through it like rice paper.

order of operations now: cedar mill cuts with the Festool, walnut crosscuts with the Festool, walnut pocket routing with the Bosch (the Bosch handles the bulk material removal in a 2x2 MDF jig), then chisel cleanup of the pocket corners (square inside corners are impossible with a round router bit, you have to chop them square with a sharp 1/4-inch chisel using paring cuts with the bevel oriented away from the cut wall). that's where the Veritas earns its keep. I ran a practice pocket on a pine scrap today using the Bosch + chisel technique and the corners came out crisp with the 1/4-inch chisel after maybe 3 paring cuts each. I'm shocked at how much progress good steel buys you.

there's a thing Don Lupe said when I was at Maderería El Roble that's lodged in my head: 'la mano aprende del fierro bueno', the hand learns from good steel. I think he was talking about hand planes specifically but it applies. cheap tools fight you and hide your real technique behind tool error; good tools reveal exactly how shaky your skills are because there's no error to blame. I'm going to be revealed by these chisels. that's fine. I want to be revealed. I want to know exactly where my hands are weak and where they're already capable, because the only path to capability is through honest feedback, and 6,200 MXN of Canadian chisel steel just bought me a feedback loop that doesn't lie.

Veritas chisels. 6-piece set, 1/4 inch through 1 inch, A2 tool steel, 25-degree primary bevel + 2-degree micro-bevel from factory, hornbeam handles, brass ferrules, etched 'V' on the blade flat. came in a wooden case with a slot for each chisel and a fitted foam liner. opened them at the kitchen table, Camila over my shoulder. she said 'son hermosos'. that's the right reaction.

lapped the backs first because Lee Valley ships them flat from factory but you still want a mirror polish on the back near the cutting edge. did 600 grit, 1000 grit, 2000 grit on a glass plate with wet-dry sandpaper. mirror finish on each one in roughly 4 minutes. then honed the bevels on a Norton 4000/8000 waterstone — I have one from Pablo's shop, borrowed for this project. each chisel took maybe 6 minutes.

tested on a pine offcut. paring cuts, with the bevel down. shavings came off translucent — you could read through them. I sliced an end-grain test cut on a 1-inch wide pine block, cleanly, with one push, no splintering. that was the moment I let myself enjoy the tools. before that the price was sitting in my mind. after that it dropped out and what remained was: this is the right tool, my hands are going to learn to use it, and the chess board pockets are going to have crisp 90-degree corners because of these chisels.

órale, also, the chisels make me realize my hand technique is bad. I'm pushing too hard with my dominant hand and not stabilizing enough with my left. I watched 90 minutes of Paul Sellers on YouTube tonight and now I know the chest-support paring technique where your wrist locks and your body weight does the cut. tomorrow I practice that on a piece of cedar offcut from Don Lupe. my hands are about to be revealed by these chisels. that's fine. I want to be revealed. I want to know exactly where my hands are weak and where they're already capable, because the only path to capability is through honest feedback, and 6,200 MXN of Canadian chisel steel just bought me a feedback loop that doesn't lie.

funny moment: Camila picked up the 1/4-inch chisel and held it the way you hold a fountain pen, said 'this is a beautiful object, it's also a deadly object, you remember that, you respect that'. she's right. these are tools that can take a thumb off in one slip. I have respected sharp things since I was 12 — dad worked at his bench every Saturday morning of my childhood with a single rusty plane and a chisel and a beat-up wooden mallet, and the rule was 'do not touch dad's chisel'. that rule still operates inside me. I'm 33 and I am mildly afraid of these chisels in exactly the way I should be. that's the right calibration of respect.

órale, also — building a list of safety habits because I want to write them down before I get casual:
1) chisel always points away from any part of my body, including the off-hand which I forget about
2) cutting board on a non-slip mat, I bought a silicone mat from the kitchen-supply place on Av. Cuauhtémoc
3) sharpen at the start of every session, not at the end of the previous one — dull chisels are how thumbs get cut, the dull chisel slips because it requires force, force makes it skip, skip lands in flesh
4) stop when tired. period. I noticed today after 90 minutes my hands were starting to ache and my paring cuts got sloppy. that's the signal
5) I am not allowed to use the chisels with mezcal in the system. zero tolerance. Camila and I have an explicit agreement on this — Saturday afternoons are workshop time, mezcal is for after dinner only on workshop days
6) Nube is allowed in the workshop area only on a leash anchored to the leg of the dining table, where she can lie on the rug but cannot wander into a foot-traffic zone with a sharp tool in motion. she has accepted this. mostly she sleeps.
7) every time a chisel comes out of its slot in the case, I say its name out loud — '1/4 inch coming out' — for muscle memory of which tool is in my hand. that one I copied from a YouTube woodworker who had been doing it for 30 years. cheap habit, high upside.

list taped to the wall above the bench. respect calibrated.

spent Saturday afternoon practicing dado pockets on pine scrap with the Bosch + Veritas chisel combo. 12 practice pockets. first 3 were rough — uneven Bosch passes left a wavy bottom because I was being too aggressive with the depth increments, and my chisel paring left visible step marks where I hadn't kept the bevel properly aligned with the pocket wall. by pocket 6 I'd figured out that the Bosch needs a single-direction pass with very light depth increments — 2mm per pass, not 4mm, and the bit wants to cut on the pull stroke which means I have to drag, not push. The chisel needs to enter the corner with the bevel facing into the wall and gently rotated rather than driven straight down — a pivot motion, like turning a key in a tight lock. By pocket 12 I was producing pockets with crisp 90-degree corners, flat bottoms, no tear-out, the kind of pocket you'd be proud to laminate a walnut square into. that's the technique I need on the actual board. I burned through maybe 2 board-feet of pine practicing. The cedar acclimation hit 14 days today. moisture meter at 6.8% and stable for 36 hours. I'm milling first cuts tomorrow morning. set the alarm for 7am. coffee tonight is decaf because I do not want to be jittery when I touch the cedar with the Festool for the first time. Camila is going to be at the office for a Pritzker portfolio review tomorrow morning so I'll have the apartment to myself until 1pm. perfect. Nube will be on the rug. I will have one shot at the first cut and I want it clean.

12 practice pockets done. last 4 were within 0.1mm of target depth and tolerance. hand technique improving — wrist locked, body weight does the cut. Camila is going to be at the office for a Pritzker portfolio review tomorrow morning so I'll have the apartment to myself until 1pm. perfect. Nube will be on the rug. I will have one shot at the first cut on the cedar with the Festool and I want it clean. setting an early alarm — 6am — coffee first, then a single deep breath, then plunge. closing the workshop for tonight, dust mask off, hands tired in the good way. I am the kind of person who gets to do this with his evenings now. that's a new shape.

first cedar cut at 7:42am Sunday. clean. like glass. I sat on the back step and stared at it for 2 minutes with my coffee getting cold next to me. plunged the Festool through 22 inches of quartersawn cedar and the kerf was 2.6mm exactly and the cut surface was so clean it didn't need sanding. cedar smell hit hard, the whole apartment got a perfume layer. Nube came over to investigate, sneezed, walked away. one cut down. nine to go for the rip-and-crosscut sequence. I am inside the project now in a way I wasn't yesterday.

órale. one cut down. nine to go for the rip-and-crosscut sequence. I am inside the project now in a way I wasn't yesterday. closing the saw and walking Nube before the next forecasted storm rolls in.

all 10 cuts done by 11:30am. cedar is now in: 2 large rectangular sections that will be edge-glued to form the 22 x 22 x 1-inch base, 4 narrower strips for the border, and a small offcut I kept for testing the inlay technique on actual material rather than pine. the walnut block is broken down into a single 2-inch wide × 16-inch long blank, ready to be crosscut into 32 little 2 x 2 squares — that's a Festool job I'll do tomorrow with a stop block on the guide rail for repeatable cuts to the same length. cleanup took longer than the cuts themselves — cedar shavings everywhere, the kind of shavings that smell so good you don't want to sweep them up. saved a small jar of fresh cedar shavings on the kitchen counter as potpourri, Camila approved when she got home from the office and immediately picked up the jar and inhaled. tomorrow: walnut crosscuts. day after: edge glue the cedar base. day after that: route the walnut pockets. that's the schedule for the next 3 days, and I'm at week 4 of a 12-week project. ahead of schedule. that's a sentence I love writing about a project. I am chasing the dopamine of an on-track Gantt and woodworking is delivering. I should journal this — sitting on the kitchen step at 11:35am with two halves of a cedar plank on the workbench and the morning sun coming in over the kitchen sink and Nube watching me from the rug — because in 8 weeks when I am stressed about glue-ups I will want to remember that this is also part of the project.

all 10 cuts done by 11:30am. cedar in: 2 large rectangular sections that will be edge-glued to form the 22 x 22 x 1-inch base, 4 narrower strips for the border, and a small offcut I kept for testing. clamped the 2 base sections with PVA and 4 quick clamps, 24-hour cure, set the kitchen timer because I am a person who timestamps wood now. wiped the squeeze-out before it cured because Don Lupe said wiping wet PVA squeeze-out before it cures is the difference between a clean glue-up and 4 hours of scraping next week.

remember this — sitting on the kitchen step at 11:35am with two halves of a cedar plank on the workbench and the morning sun coming in over the kitchen sink and Nube watching me from the rug — because in 8 weeks when I am stressed about glue-ups I will want to remember that this is also part of the project.

I screwed up. need to write this out properly because the lesson is real and I want to remember exactly how it happened, including the specific mental error I made, so I can audit for it on the next mistake which will be different but related.

context: today was edge-glue day for the cedar base. I had two cedar sections (22 x 11 x 1 inch each), I was going to glue them edge-to-edge to form the 22 x 22 x 1 base. Standard edge-glue technique: get the mating edges perfectly square (which means jointing them with a hand plane or running them along the Festool guide rail at exactly 90 degrees), run a thin bead of Titebond III along the joint, clamp across the joint with parallel-jaw clamps for 24 hours under even pressure, wipe squeeze-out with a damp rag while it's still wet. easy. I'd practiced on pine. I felt confident. the first cuts went well. the chisels are sharp. the workspace is dialed.

except.

when I put the two cedar sections side by side this morning, ready to glue, I noticed something off. the grain pattern on the two sections wasn't matching. one section had the cedar's growth rings going horizontally through the thickness (quartersawn — the way Don Lupe specced it, vertical lines on the face, horizontal lines through the thickness), but the other section had the rings going closer to vertical through the thickness, more like riftsawn or even partial flatsawn.

it took me a full 90 seconds of staring before I realized what had happened: I'd placed one of the sections upside-down during the rip cuts. when I did the original cut on the Festool, I placed plank A grain-up and plank B grain-down without thinking — I just laid them flat on the workbench in the order I'd grabbed them from the acclimation stack, didn't note orientation, didn't mark them. when I cut, I produced two 22-inch sections that weren't actually orientation-matched. one came out of plank A face-up, the other came out of plank B face-down, and when I flipped both to glue them I was looking at one face and one back, with grain orientations rotated 180 degrees relative to each other.

now: this matters. the grain orientation determines how the wood expands and contracts with seasonal humidity. a quartersawn section barely changes width. a flatsawn section can change up to 4% across the grain over the year. if I glue a quartersawn piece to a flatsawn piece, the joint will fight itself with seasonal humidity changes — the flatsawn piece will swell sideways, the quartersawn piece won't, and over time the joint cracks. for a chess board that's going to live in 70% rainy-season CDMX humidity and then potentially travel to São Paulo (also high humidity, also seasonal), this is not a forgivable mistake. it would crack within 2 years. I would be giving Hugo a chess board with a planned obsolescence date.

I sat down on the back step and wanted to cry, briefly. then I called Pablo because Pablo is calm in the face of dumb problems and he doesn't perform sympathy. he picked up, I explained the mistake in detail, he said 'no manches Seb, lo replaneas, tienes madera de sobra'. and he was right. I had the third plank Don Lupe sold me as 'por si la riegas'. the spare. I had bought it specifically for this scenario. I just hadn't recognized I was in the scenario yet.

solution: I'm going to re-rip from the spare plank, this time being meticulous about grain orientation. I'm going to mark each plank with a Sharpie before cutting — top edge of grain, bottom edge of grain, with arrows pointing in the direction of the grain rise — so when I lay them on the workbench I have a visual confirmation of orientation that survives across multiple cut sessions. then I'm going to cut both 22-inch sections from the same plank, top half and bottom half, so they're guaranteed to be grain-matched because they came from the same board with the same growth-ring profile. one of the original two sections from yesterday I'll keep for practice routing. the other I'll re-purpose for the border strips, which are smaller and where grain orientation matters less because the border strips don't need to expand-and-contract in match with the main slab.

cost of mistake: I lose half a day of progress, plus the spare plank gets used (which was always its purpose, so no real material loss), plus I have to re-mill which means the Festool comes back out and I do another sequence of cuts. probably 90 minutes of work to recover. emotional cost: I felt stupid for about 2 hours and then I got over it because Pablo was on the phone laughing at me good-naturedly and Camila came home and gave me a look that said 'estás aprendiendo' and made me a tea (manzanilla, with honey, the kind she makes for the back-tweak nights too).

lesson, written out properly: in software I would have caught this with a code review or a unit test or a type check. in woodworking the equivalent is the marking step — you mark the wood before you cut so you can audit yourself, and you photograph the marked wood with your phone before cutting so you have a record. I'm building a workshop habit now of: orient the plank, mark the orient with arrow on the face and back, photograph (yes, photograph, with phone, for the project record), then cut. mark twice, photo once, cut once, the woodworker's joke per ChatGPT or whatever. I'm laminating the new orientation rule into my mental checklist alongside 'wear safety glasses' and 'keep dust extraction running'.

re-milling tomorrow morning, fresh approach, 90 minutes of work, and I'll be back where I thought I was today. the mistake delays me 24 hours net. I'm at week 4 of 12 still, on schedule. fine. expensive mistakes early are cheaper than expensive mistakes late, this is a rule that applies to backend systems and chess boards apparently. picking myself up. eating dinner. resetting. Camila is putting on a record — she pulled the Caetano 'Transa' from the shelf, which means she's pulling out the comfort vinyl for me. she knows. five years and she still beats me to my own emotional weather. tea is still warm. moving on.

the error: I oriented the grain of the 2 cedar base sections perpendicular to each other instead of parallel. cedar moves across grain with humidity. if you glue 2 panels at 90-degree grain orientation, one panel expands as the other contracts, and the glue line either fails (best case) or the panel cracks (worst case). I knew this. Don Lupe explicitly told me this 3 weeks ago at Maderería El Roble. he said 'orienta la veta paralela en cada tabla del panel base.' he said the words. parallel grain. I wrote it down. I underlined it. then this morning at 7am I clamped the sections together and went to make coffee and Camila came in and looked at the panel and said 'amor, esa veta está perpendicular'. I looked. she was right. I had glued two perpendicular-grain panels because I'd flipped one section thinking the visual face match looked better with that orientation. visual aesthetics overrode structural rules. classic beginner mistake.

spent 25 minutes deciding whether to try to save it (re-rip and re-glue) or scrap it (mill the spare plank into 2 new sections, re-orient parallel, re-glue). chose scrap. couldn't trust the glue line under inevitable CDMX humidity swings. spare plank is 1.5x what I need so the math works. lost ~40 USD of cedar and 4 hours of work. lesson cost: noted.

the meta-lesson: I'm not going to be a 'good enough' woodworker by reading internet forums. I'm going to be a 'good enough' woodworker by making mistakes that I cannot undo without re-buying material, which is exactly the kind of mistake that gets remembered. cedar is forgiving in some ways, unforgiving in this way. write it on the wall: GRAIN ORIENTATION FIRST, AESTHETICS SECOND. ALWAYS.

the parallel to software: I make this same mistake at work in a different costume. I optimize for the readable diff over the structurally-correct diff. I prioritize the merge-on-Friday over the post-mortem-proof Tuesday-design. I orient for the appearance of progress instead of the durability of the thing. it's a known failure mode for me and I have been working on it for years. cedar is just code with grain. one of these days I will internalize that the structural rule is the rule, period.

I texted Don Lupe a photo of the failed glue-up and a one-line note: 'me equivoqué con la veta. estoy moliendo la tabla de respaldo'. he replied 30 minutes later: 'todos lo hacemos la primera vez. ahora ya nunca lo olvidarás. saludos.' that is the kindest reply I have received from anyone who is technically a vendor. it cost him nothing and it cost me nothing and it landed exactly where it needed to land. I am going to call him next week about the maple I'm going to need for the chess pieces, and the call is going to be 30% wood selection and 70% checking in on a man who lost his wife in 2018 and is mostly alone with his lumber. I think that's part of the deal of buying from Don Lupe.

órale. dinner. resetting. Camila is putting on a record — she pulled the Caetano 'Transa' from the shelf, which means she's pulling out the comfort vinyl for me. she knows. five years and she still beats me to my own emotional weather. tea is still warm. moving on.

re-mill done. spare plank rip-cut into 2 grain-matched 22-inch sections, top and bottom halves of the same board, marked carefully with arrows in pencil before cutting, photographed at three angles before the saw came out. orientation is identical now between the two halves and I confirmed this morning by holding them up to the side window light at 45 degrees and watching the grain catch evenly. I edge-glued them this afternoon — Titebond III bead applied with a small acid brush in a continuous line, parallel jaw clamps, 4 of them across the 22-inch length, even pressure tightened in alternating sequence (clamp 1, clamp 4, clamp 2, clamp 3), glue squeeze-out which is the sign of a good joint. wiped the squeeze-out with a damp rag and a putty knife. clamped for 24 hours. doing nothing till tomorrow afternoon, the boards are off-limits to Nube and to the cleaning lady who comes Wednesday. Opened a small mezcal to celebrate the recovery, sat with Camila on the kitchen step. she said 'aprendiste algo' and I said yes. Small victory. The Veritas chisels are watching from the bench, lined up on a strip of leather she hung off the wall as a holster. I think they approve. I think Don Lupe would approve. Tomorrow afternoon I unclamp.

Tomorrow afternoon I unclamp. tonight I sleep with the satisfaction of having recovered from a beginner mistake without crying about it on the internet — just took the L, milled the spare, glued correctly, moved on. that is woodworking discipline I did not have 5 weeks ago. closing the workshop, dog walk, dinner, sleep. mate first.

edge-glue cured 24 hours. unclamped this morning at 10:14am, set the kitchen timer for it because I wanted the full 24 — beautiful joint, completely invisible from above, cedar to cedar, grain flow continuous, no glue line visible. ran a hand plane across the joint to verify flatness — the cabinet scraper picked up a tiny ridge of dried glue squeeze-out and shaved it off cleanly in two passes. flat. solid. now I have a 22 x 22 x 1-inch cedar slab, the chess board base, and it weighs about 3.5 kilos which is more than I expected and feels good when you pick it up — feels like a real object. Camila walked past, lifted it with both hands, and said 'oh wow es pesado'. yes. that's right. that's the gift starting to be a thing.

next step is laying out the 8x8 grid for the walnut pocket routing — using a combination square and a hard pencil, gridding off 2-inch squares with thin pencil lines, adjusting the layout so the playable area is dead-centered with a 2-inch border on all sides. I'm going to spend 90 minutes on this layout because if the grid is off by 0.5mm in one direction the whole board reads as off and there's no way to fix it after the routing starts.

then I'll route the alternating squares to a depth of 6mm using the Bosch + a 2x2 jig I'm going to build tonight from MDF scrap. 32 pockets total. gonna take maybe 4 hours of careful routing across 2 days. then the chisel work to square the corners with the 1/4-inch Veritas. then the walnut inlay glue-up in batches of 8 inlays per night, 4 nights total. then sanding flush. that's weeks 5-7 of the project. on track. cedar dust everywhere. nose itches in a good way.

ok full design spec dump for the chess board because I want it on record before I forget the reasoning, and I want to be able to refer back to this when I'm 4 weeks deeper in and second-guessing every decision under the influence of some new YouTube video. this is the chess board for Hugo Ferreira sr's 70th birthday gift. delivery date: Hugo's 70th, which is in roughly 8 weeks from this writing.

recipient: Hugo Ferreira sr, retired civil engineer, 70 years old, widowed since month 0 of this arc when Beatriz died of pancreatic cancer in São Paulo, currently visiting CDMX monthly because Camila is here and so am I and there's no one in São Paulo for him beyond a Brazilian engineer chess buddy named Tato and a Tuesday-bridge group that dissolved after Beatriz's death. recipient context relevant to design: hands shake slightly with age, plays casually but seriously, learned chess at 14 from his civil-engineering uncle, taught Camila at 8, currently uses a beat-up plastic travel set with a magnetic snap closure, would never buy himself a real board because he doesn't think he plays seriously enough to deserve one.

design intent: respond to recipient context. board has to be usable for him as he is, not as he was at 50. magnetic pieces because his hands shake — pieces won't tip during a long thinking pause. 20-inch full-tournament size because he plays full tournament games online (mostly correspondence-style chess on chess.com against Tato) and needs the dimensions to feel familiar. Cedar base because cedar smells like São Paulo for him (peroba is what his old neighborhood smelled like and it's in the same family of aromatic woods, close enough to connect for someone with that olfactory memory). walnut squares because the contrast with cedar is warm rather than stark — I considered ebony for the dark squares which is the traditional choice for high-end boards but ebony is jet-black and the visual register is cold; ebony would have read as an Ikea showroom board, which is wrong for Hugo. walnut is brown-black with subtle figure, much warmer, and it ages with the cedar over years rather than against it.

Quartersawn cedar because rainy-season CDMX hits 70% humidity and São Paulo similar, flatsawn cedar would warp a millimeter or two and ruin the playable surface within 2 seasons. I learned this the hard way last week with the grain-orientation mistake, which was a free lesson in why it matters.

dimensions: outer board 20 x 20 inches, playable area 16 x 16 inches in 8x8 squares of 2 inches each, border 2 inches all around, total thickness 1 inch, walnut inlay depth 6mm, leaving 19mm of cedar under each walnut square as base. material spec: cedro rojo (Cedrela odorata) sourced from Maderería El Roble in Lerma, Toluca, from Don Lupe — quartersawn, 12% kiln-dried, acclimated to 6.8% in CDMX over 14 days. walnut from same source, sound, no defects, grain-matched within the block.

construction: edge-glued cedar slab (Titebond III, parallel jaw clamps, 24-hour cure), routed walnut pockets (Bosch GMR 1 plunge router with 2x2 MDF jig, 6mm depth, light passes 2mm at a time, single-direction passes), chiseled corners square (Veritas 1/4-inch chisel, paring cuts, bevel rotated into the pocket wall), walnut inlays glued in (Titebond III, light pressure, 24-hour cure each course of 8 inlays), sanded flush (cabinet scraper then 220 grit then 320 grit then 400 grit, sanding-with-the-grain-only on the final pass), beeswax finish (Howard Feed-N-Wax, applied in 3 coats over 3 days, buffed with cotton between coats with 24-hour wait between for cure).

hardware: rubber feet inset on the back at the four corners (3M industrial adhesive, set into routed recesses so they sit flush with the back surface). monogram: 'HFC' in serif font, beneath which is '70', etched lightly with a wood-burning pen, on the back of the board centered, done as the last step before final beeswax pass.

piece spec: 32 chess pieces, 16 white maple, 16 black walnut, magnetic bases (5mm neodymium magnets seated in 3D-printed cylindrical bases at Atomic Print fab shop in Roma Sur, then upper piece glued on top of the base with cyanoacrylate). pieces to be standard Staunton style, downloaded from the open-source Staunton STL model on Thingiverse, scaled to tournament regulation height (king 95mm, pawn 50mm). the white maple I'll source from Don Lupe on a follow-up trip in week 7. the magnets I've ordered already (300 MXN for 50 of them, oversupply for safety).

cost analysis: cedar 1.6k MXN, walnut 1.2k MXN, Festool TS 55 REQ 14.8k MXN (reusable, 20-year tool), Bosch GMR 1 2.4k MXN (reusable), Veritas chisels 6.2k MXN (reusable, 30-year tools), Titebond III 250 MXN (the 8oz bottle), beeswax 200 MXN, magnets 300 MXN, 3D print fab 600 MXN, maple 800 MXN (estimate from Don Lupe pricing on similar hardwoods), miscellaneous (sandpaper, MDF for jigs, cabinet scraper, pencils, wood-burning pen) 500 MXN. total project cost: 28.85k MXN, of which 23.4k is reusable tooling and 5.45k is consumed materials.

risk register: (1) wrong grain orientation — caught at week 4, mitigated with spare plank, this happened, lesson learned, marking protocol now in place. (2) walnut pockets coming out uneven from routing — mitigated with practice on pine, MDF jig, light-pass technique, 12 practice pockets. (3) glue-up failure (delamination) — mitigated with Titebond III high-tack, parallel-jaw clamps, even pressure protocol. (4) warping post-glue from humidity — mitigated by quartersawn material, beeswax sealing on all 6 surfaces including the back, full perimeter sealed before delivery. (5) magnetic base falling off the upper piece — mitigated by CA glue with primer, plus a small structural pin if needed. (6) the gift not landing emotionally — unmitigatable, Hugo will either love it or feel obligated to love it and I won't be able to tell which, and Camila will tell me later in the privacy of our apartment.

timeline: weeks 1-2 acclimation (done), week 3 setup (done), week 4 milling (done), week 4 grain mistake + recovery (done), week 5 routing pockets (current), week 6 walnut inlay glue-up, week 7 sanding and surface prep + Don Lupe maple trip, week 8 piece preparation and 3D print run at Atomic Print, week 9 magnetic base assembly, week 10 finishing (beeswax 3-coat), week 11 hardware (feet, monogram), week 12 final assembly and presentation rehearsal with Camila at the apartment.

8 weeks left. dad would approve of this spec. dad has played chess for 50 years and would specifically approve of the 20-inch tournament size and the magnetic accommodation for tremor. I'll send him the photos when it's done. probably bring the board to Guadalajara at Christmas and let him see it live before it goes to Hugo. closing the spec for now. printing this thread to a Notion page tomorrow so I have a permanent reference.

full design spec for the chess board, version final, for record:

Dimensions: cedar base 558.8mm x 558.8mm x 25.4mm thick. Border 25.4mm wide. Total 609.6mm x 609.6mm x 25.4mm. Mass approximately 2.1 kg.

Materials: cedar (cedro rojo) from Maderería El Roble, Toluca, single-tree quartersawn lumber. Walnut for inlays from same supplier, 5-year air-dried. Beeswax + mineral oil 1:3 ratio for finish.

Surface treatment: 8x8 grid of inlays, 50.8mm squares. Light squares = bare cedar. Dark squares = walnut inlay, 6mm deep in routed pockets, glued with PVA, sanded flush. Tournament-standard light/dark color contrast 70:30, achieved by walnut natural color against pale cedar.

Piece accommodation: magnetic. 32 chess pieces (Staunton design, hollow base). Each piece base receives a 3D-printed PLA insert with embedded N52 neodymium magnet, polarity-aligned. Each board square receives a 5mm steel disk inset 5mm below cedar surface, hidden under a 1.5mm cedar veneer cap. Holds piece against board through tremor or table bump. Test plan: check magnet pull strength against piece weight + 30g table-bump tolerance.

Finish protocol: 220-grit final sand. Apply beeswax warm with cotton cloth. Cure 4 hours. Buff with horsehair. Apply second coat. Cure 4 hours. Buff. Apply third coat. Cure 24 hours. Final buff with soft cotton.

Packaging: cedar storage box, hinged, lined with green felt. Travel sleeve in waxed canvas. Total presentation weight ~3.2 kg.

timeline now: weeks 5-7 routing/chiseling/walnut inlay glue-ups. weeks 8-9 sanding and border attach. weeks 10 piece printing (Pablo's shop) + magnet assembly. week 11 finishing. week 12 box and pre-flight. delivery target: Hugo's 70th birthday party in CDMX, exactly 7 weeks from this Saturday.

cost breakdown for record: cedar planks from Don Lupe ~1850 MXN, walnut blocks ~600 MXN, Festool track saw 22500 MXN, Bosch trim router 3200 MXN, Veritas chisels 6200 MXN, jig MDF + clamps 700 MXN, beeswax + oil 280 MXN, 3D print + magnets through Pablo at cost 350 MXN, hinged box materials 400 MXN, waxed canvas sleeve 500 MXN. project-direct cost: ~3800 MXN. tool acquisition: ~32k MXN. for accounting purposes the tools are amortized across future projects, the project itself is in the noise.

dad will see this board first, before Hugo does. I'm going to bring it to Guadalajara at Christmas. dad will quietly understand the magnetic accommodation for tremor and the dimension choice for the travel sleeve. dad himself doesn't have hand tremor but he watched his uncle play chess with one for years before the uncle died and he'll recognize the design choice. he plays chess. he will understand the board. he will not say much about it and he will be deeply moved. that's how dad receives objects. that's how I learned to make objects.

things I will not put on the board: my initials. a date. any inscription. the board has to be a board, not a monument with my name on it. Hugo can have it inscribed by his own woodworker in São Paulo if he wants. or never. either is fine. the gift is the object, not the signature on the object. I'm explicitly not signing it. that's a deliberate choice and Camila agrees.

things I will put on the bottom of the storage box: a small leather tag with the wood source ('cedro rojo, Maderería El Roble, Toluca, MX') and the year ('2026'). that's not a signature, it's a provenance label. Hugo will appreciate provenance. he's an engineer. engineers want to know where the wood came from.

edge case I'm thinking through: what if the customs delay on the magnets through DHL pushes the build past Hugo's birthday. backup: I have a 6-week buffer. magnets are due in 4 weeks. if DHL holds them at customs (it has happened, Pablo had a 3D printer parts saga in 2024), I switch to the local Tepito magnet supplier with lower-pull N42s and ship anyway. backup plan documented.

edge case 2: what if dad sees the board at Christmas and wants one too. honestly, I'm half-hoping for this. I'd build dad a board in 2027. different wood — probably mesquite, because dad's family grew up in Sonora and mesquite has the same gut-punch cedar has for Hugo. project for a different year.

started routing walnut pockets today. built the 2x2 MDF jig last night — it's a square frame with internal opening of exactly 50.8mm (2 inches) which guides the Bosch around the perimeter so the bit cuts a clean 2-inch square pocket every time, with the bit traveling around the inside edge of the frame. clamped the jig to the cedar slab over the first marked square (a1, dark square, walnut goes there, pencil-marked from the grid layout), set the Bosch depth stop to 6mm, made 3 light passes at 2mm each. clean pocket, slight tear-out at the corners which I expected. moved the jig to the next dark square (b2, on the diagonal because the dark squares are diagonal), repeated. by pocket 6 I had a rhythm — clamp the jig, plunge, traverse, lift, unclamp, slide, re-clamp on the next square. by pocket 10 the Bosch was getting hot — small router, can't run continuous, so I gave it a 5-minute rest break and Nube and I went for a walk to the corner. came back, did 10 more. 20 pockets done by mid-afternoon. switched to the Veritas 1/4-inch chisel and squared up the inside corners on those 20 — paring cuts, bevel down, very gentle, wrist-rotation rather than push. the chisel earns its 1.2k MXN per chisel cost in this exact step. corners came out crisp. 12 more pockets to go tomorrow. cedar dust accumulated on the floor of the terrace in a fragrant little pile. I kept it for a sachet.

interesting parallel between the chess board work and the rust migration that I've been mulling over the past three days, and writing it out here so I remember it. both are precision projects with a long timeline (12 weeks for the board, 18 months for the migration) and a clear deliverable (a finished object, a service in prod). both involve choosing the right tool — Festool/Veritas vs tokio/sqlx — and committing capital up front so the later work isn't fighting tool error later in the project. both have the same recovery dynamic when something goes wrong: catch it early, isolate the bad piece, replace from a spare or refactor cleanly, don't let the mistake propagate through the rest of the project. the cedar grain mistake last week is structurally identical to the Tokio task-spawn leak two months ago — both were silent failures that would have ruined a downstream system if they had survived to delivery. both got caught by a deliberate audit step (visual orientation check vs pprof analysis). both required an 'I had a spare for exactly this' recovery (spare plank from Don Lupe, refactor budget from Roberto). I think this is why I keep coming back to woodworking even though I'm a backend engineer. the discipline transfers in a deep way I didn't expect. the patience builds the same muscle. Pablo joked at Atlas yesterday between rounds that I'm 'becoming the kind of person who has hobbies', meaning hobbies that aren't just side projects on a screen, and I think he's right and it's been a slow conversion. my back can't take 5 sparring rounds anymore at 50% PT recovery, but I can spend 3 hours at a workbench and feel my body settle in a way I haven't since the kickboxing was uncomplicated. that's a different kind of fitness.

the parallel: rust migration is replacing a Python service that's been running for 3 years and sort of works, with a faster cleaner thing. chess board is making an object that will last 50 years and is a kind of personal monument. both are precision projects with a long lead time and no shortcut. both reward patience and punish ego. both have a moment where you realize you've been doing something wrong for 3 hours and have to undo and redo. both have a moment where the tools work and you pause to acknowledge them. both are the kind of work I'm starting to believe I'd do for free.

Pablo said something last weekend at the gym: 'tu mejor trabajo no es el de la pantalla, primo'. your best work isn't the work on a screen. I think he's right and it's been a slow conversion. my back can't take 5 sparring rounds anymore at 50% PT recovery, but I can spend 3 hours at a workbench and feel my body settle in a way I haven't since the kickboxing was uncomplicated. that's a different kind of fitness. and unlike the screen work, the workbench work I have a continuous body memory of — I can close my eyes and remember the exact resistance of cedar against the Veritas in a way I cannot remember any single line of Rust I have written this year.
