The cedar slab has 32 routed pockets in it now. I keep reaching for it on the workbench and lifting it just to feel the weight in my hands. It is becoming an object. I am becoming the person who made an object.

I've been a backend engineer for 12 years. I've been a serious kickboxer for 4 years. I've been a vegetarian for 4 years too — same trigger date as the kickboxing, the year of dad's bypass, the year I watched the surgical debrief video and made a decision in one night that has held. I've been with Camila for 5 years. I've been in CDMX for almost 3 years. I'm 33 and a half. and the chess board has revealed something about how I've been operating in my career and life that I want to name.

for years I've defaulted to the digital. backend systems, side projects on GitHub, code reviews, pprof traces, slack threads, Notion docs. The work is meaningful, it pays well — 95k USD eq plus the promo bump in month 2 — and it's mine in some real sense. But the work has no physical residue. when I shipped the rust mpsc fix three weeks ago, the only artifact was a PR diff and a metrics dashboard. when the migration ships to staging in 4 weeks the artifact will be a Notion document and a green check mark in CI. there is no plank. there is no thing you can pick up.

the chess board project has produced — in 5 weeks — a 22 x 22-inch slab of edge-glued cedar with 32 walnut pockets routed into it. this thing has weight. I picked it up yesterday morning to move it to a different bench and it weighs about 3.5 kilos and I felt something in my spine adjust to the load. when Hugo opens this in 8 weeks he is going to put his hands on cedar I sourced from Don Lupe in Toluca, walnut I cut to size with a Festool I had to convince myself to buy, edges chiseled square with Veritas chisels I sharpened on Naniwa stones at my kitchen table while Camila played Caetano in the background and Nube slept on the bath mat. there is no equivalent of this in 12 years of code. I've made software that handles a million requests a day in production and I have less physical evidence of that work than I do of one cedar slab that took me 5 weekends.

that's a fact about the medium, not a complaint about the work. but I notice it. I notice that the way I felt when I unclamped the cedar slab a week ago Tuesday was the same way I felt when I unwrapped the bombilla cup abuela Lupita gave me in 2017, the same feeling of 'this is now part of the world, and I am part of it because I touched it.' I don't get that from a deploy. I never have. and lately, since the rust memory leak got fixed and the tokio refactor stabilized and the Bogotá decline went out and Mateo's engagement happened and Nube's thunder phobia got managed and the trip to Tijuana got planned, I've had this quiet expansion in my chest about what kinds of work I want to do for the rest of my life.

the pose-est project is paused since month 11 and I keep coming back to it not because I want to revive it but because it was a moment when I tried to use code to capture a physical practice (kickboxing form analysis from phone video) and the gap between what I could capture digitally and what was actually happening in a sparring round haunted me. The chess board is the inverse. I'm using physical making to commemorate a relationship — Hugo's chess habit, his widowhood, his connection to Camila and now to me. and the physical making feels much more like the thing I want to be doing than capturing physicality digitally ever did. that's something. that's a clue.

it's not a career pivot — I'm not abandoning backend engineering at 33 to become a luthier in San Miguel de Allende. but it's a clue about where my next 10 years might lean. maybe MIT, where physical-digital research lives. maybe more woodworking, on the side, on weekends, building toward something. maybe both. maybe the kid-timing conversation Camila has been waiting on me for needs to fold this in — what kind of life do I want to be making, with what kind of hands, in what kind of weeks, with what kind of evenings.

I haven't said this to anyone fully. Pablo would laugh sweetly because Pablo has rebuilt three motorcycles from carb to chassis and he's been waiting for me to figure this out. Camila would understand immediately because she's an architect and she lives in this exact tension every day — software-y BIM models vs real buildings on real sites with real masons cursing at real concrete. dad would understand at the level of a chess player who has played 50 years and knows the pieces in his hands feel different than the pieces on Lichess; he's said as much in passing twice now. abuela Lupita would have understood best. she made things. she made aguascalientes lace and pickled chiles and the kind of black bean soup that you remember as a personality trait, not a recipe. she'd light a candle for the cedar. I'm lighting one for her tonight on the way home — she'd be 89 in 3 weeks. lapsed Catholic with an abuela-shaped exception, that is my entire spiritual position summarized in one phrase.

the back hurts again at the right SI joint, low-grade ache from the sparring tweak Wednesday, PT next Thursday, the new glute-activation routine is helping in week one. The insomnia is still there, hovering, 3am wake-ups, mind churning. but this evening at Cardinal with cold mate, sketchpad open, cedar dust in my fingernails I haven't quite cleaned out yet — this evening I am as awake and as aware and as physical as I have been in years. I think I'm becoming a different shape of myself. I want to remember this exact night. closing the laptop. walking home along Yucatán. there's a candle to light.

weekend reflection turn. Cardinal at 8:42pm, mate cold next to me. The cedar slab has 32 routed pockets in it now, the walnut squares are cut and waiting in a pile next to the workbench, and tomorrow I start the inlay glue-up — first 8 squares of 32. I am 5 weeks into a 12-week project. half done, more or less. that's the moment in any project where the energy turns from research-anxiety to execution-rhythm and you stop wondering if you're going to finish and start wondering what the day after the deadline feels like. that's a particular flavor of project-half satisfaction I have felt maybe 8 times in my adult life, and tonight is one of them. I want to mark it.

things I'm noticing in myself:

1) my hands are different. literally — there's a small callus on the inside of my left palm from holding the Bosch router for hours, and the chisels have left a thin line on the second knuckle of my right index finger. but also figuratively — I notice things I did not notice 5 weeks ago. wood grain. tool weight. light direction on a cut surface. yesterday I spent 2 minutes looking at the way a single shaving curled off a Veritas blade and felt at peace in a specific way I have not felt at the keyboard since the early days of pose-est in month 4.

2) I am sleeping marginally better. insomnia hasn't fully resolved but I'm getting an honest 6 hours most nights now versus 5 a month ago. theory: my brain doesn't have spare cycles to ruminate at 1am because it spent the day doing physical micro-decisions. that's a hypothesis. could also be the back PT working. could also be the chess board taking the rumination slot that the rust migration used to have.

3) I am thinking about MIT differently. when I started thinking about the Media Lab masters last month it was abstract — research career, ML for nonverbal feedback, Rosalind Picard, fancy. tonight at the bench it shifted. the work I want to do is the work where the body learns through feedback that the body can feel. kickboxing form analyzer was that. teaching Nube to recover from thunder is that. building an object Hugo will play on for 20 years is that. it's all the same shape: the object teaches the body, the body teaches the object back. the academic framing is 'embodied cognition meets affective computing' — fine, that's a personal statement angle. but the lived framing is 'I make tools that make better people'. that's the clearer thesis.

4) I love Camila more, in a weirdly geometric way, after 5 years. tonight she came up behind me at the workbench and put her hand on my shoulder and said 'es muy hermoso, Seb, estás muy presente'. I am very present. I haven't been very present in a long time. she noticed first. she always notices first. I don't know how I got this lucky. I am also aware that 'I don't know how I got this lucky' is the thing you say before something happens to test whether you actually know how lucky you are. so I am going to make sure I notice every time she's right about my emotional weather, every time, and tell her so out loud.

5) I have not heard from Bogotá since I declined. Roberto has not mentioned it. Alejandra Téllez has not emailed. Daniela texted me 'good call' the same week and that was the entire postmortem on the corporate side. the door seems to have actually closed. my body has not relapsed about it. that's the honest post-mortem on the decision: I don't miss it, not even a little, and that is more meaningful than a thousand pre-decision agonies.

6) the MIT app — I'm starting to actually believe I might apply. up until last month it was a fantasy I'd carry around in my head and not commit to. tonight it feels like a thing I'm going to commit to in writing, which is the difference. I don't know if I'll get in. probably I won't. but the application itself is the work, and the application is teaching me what I want to study. drafted the personal statement opening line tonight in my notes app while waiting for the espresso to drop: 'most physical training operates on a feedback loop the body can feel but the mind cannot describe — and most digital systems operate on the inverse.' that's the seed. I'll let it sit for a week.

7) I lit a candle for abuela Lupita this morning, even though her birthday isn't for another month. felt right. she would have laughed at me building a chess board for a Brazilian widower in CDMX. she would also have given me three pieces of advice about wood that I do not currently have, because she came from a family of mezcaleros who built oak barrels for fermentation and she knew her grain. I miss her. that is the thing under the chess board — wanting to ask abuela about the wood and not being able to.

8) Camila and I had a kid-conversation tonight at dinner that didn't end in a fight for the first time. it ended in 'we'll talk about this again in 6 months'. that is progress. one full year ago this conversation made us go to couples therapy. now it's a 'we'll table it' that doesn't carry tension. the relationship has actually moved. I don't know where it's going to land. I do know it's going to land somewhere by month 24 of arc and I'm not avoiding it the way I was at month 7.

órale. closing this turn. the chess board is going to be okay. the rust migration is going to ship. mom is healthy. dad is alive. abuela's mate cup is on the windowsill at home with last night's mate stem still in it. Nube is at the apartment under Camila's feet. La Polar is open in 2 hours and the cumbia will start. Doña Marta will start the bread oven in 8.5 hours. I am 33 years old, I have all my fingers, my eyes work, I drank good mate today, my hands smell like cedar, my back is at 50% but climbing, my brain has cycles to think, my partner is asleep on the couch with a book on her chest, my dog is unafraid of the air conditioner for the first time in 6 weeks. there is so much that is good and so much that I forget to thank the universe for, and tonight at the workbench with cedar dust in my fingernails I haven't quite cleaned out yet — this evening I am as awake and as aware and as physical as I have been in years. I think I'm becoming a different shape of myself. I want to remember this exact night. closing the laptop. walking home along Yucatán. there's a candle to light.

final note for tonight before I close: I'm grateful. that's the actual emotion under all the planning and the spec docs and the lists. I'm grateful for cedar and chisels and a partner who edits my emotional weather and a dog who is learning to be unafraid and a brother getting married and parents who are alive and a city that smells like rain on concrete in June. that's enough. closing.
